title: Long-Exhale Breath, Six Rounds
subtitle: A three-minute breath practice for fast settling when anxiety is high
excerpt: A three-minute breath practice. Six rounds of in-breath through the nose and a longer out-breath through the mouth. The simplest way to turn the parasympathetic brake on when the body has gone into alarm.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The long-out-breath practice draws on pranayama traditions and on the modern adaptations made by Andrew Weil and others.

practice.whenToUse: Use in a live anxiety moment, in the car before a meeting, in the bathroom of a busy event, or in any moment the body has tipped into alarm and needs a fast reset.

content.text: A three-minute breath practice. Six rounds of in-breath through the nose and a longer out-breath through the mouth. Used to settle the body fast when the alarm has fired.
Sit or stand. Eyes open or soft. The shoulders can drop. No special posture needed.
Round one. Breathe in slowly through the nose for a count of four. Breathe out through the mouth for a count of six. The out-breath is longer than the in-breath. That is the whole shape.
Round two. Same shape. In for four, out for six. The counting is the work; let the body do the breathing.
Rounds three and four. Same shape. If the out-breath can go longer, let it. In for four, out for eight is also workable.
Rounds five and six. Slow. By round six the body has usually changed gear. The heart is slower. The chest has softened.
Let the breath return to its own rhythm. Take one more slow breath. Carry on with the next thing.
If the alarm is still loud, run another six rounds. The practice does not have a daily limit.
Original to homemade.education. The long-out-breath shape comes from pranayama traditions. Modern teachers like Andrew Weil have built on it for general use.
